ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for peanut chaat as a composite dish. Mapped to 'peanut food product' (FOODON_03315873) as the closest defensible broad anchor. Composite chaat preparation with yoghurt and sauces is not individually represented in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers peanuts/groundnuts as a commodity. No composite chaat preparation code exists in FoodEx2; broad anchor used for the primary ingredient.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for peanut chaat as a composite prepared dish. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual regional snack preparations at this specificity.

preparation_method: dressed with tomato sauce and homemade chaat masala sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of peanut chaat is approximately 200 g, typically presented as a bowl or plate containing roasted or boiled peanuts dressed with yoghurt, onion, tomato sauce, and chaat masala.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted peanuts and composite chaat preparation — estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) peanut entries and USDA FoodData Central groundnut data, combined with proportional adjustment for yoghurt, onion, and sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition); USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for peanut chaat as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 roasted groundnut data (primary anchor, ~100 g peanuts per 200 g serving) combined with proportional contributions from plain yoghurt (USDA FDC #171284), raw onion (USDA FDC #170000), and tomato-based sauce (~20 g). Macros weighted by approximate ingredient proportions: peanuts 50%, yoghurt 25%, onion 10%, sauces 15%. Values represent a reasonable central estimate; actual restaurant preparation will vary.

gi_source.title: Peanuts, roasted — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; composite dish GI estimated by weighted-ingredient method
gi_source.source_match_notes: Roasted peanuts have a published GI of approximately 14–23 (University of Sydney GI Database; Foster-Powell et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2002). Plain yoghurt GI ~36. Onion and tomato sauce contribute minimal glycemic load. Weighted composite GI estimated at ~23 (low category) based on peanut dominance (~50% by weight) and low-GI yoghurt component. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (23 × 18.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2) / 100 = 8.4 g. Classified as low GI (≤55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from sauces and chaat masala
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g serving of peanut chaat contributes approximately 420 mg sodium, primarily from tomato sauce and chaat masala seasoning; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce preparations and monitor cumulative daily sodium intake against the recommended limit of 2000 mg/day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by peanut and yoghurt components
type2_diabetes.guidance: Peanut chaat has an estimated low GI of approximately 23 and a glycemic load of ~8.4 per 200 g serving, making it a suitable occasional snack for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though the yoghurt and sauce components add modest carbohydrate and sugar that should be counted within the daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial unsaturated fats from peanuts offset by moderate saturated fat and sodium from sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Peanuts provide heart-beneficial monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats (~16.9 g per 100 g combined) and are associated with reduced LDL cholesterol; however, the sauce and seasoning components add saturated fat and sodium, so individuals with cardiovascular disease should favour preparations with minimal added sauce and consume as part of a diet low in saturated fat and sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peanut chaat (also spelled 'peanut chat') is a popular North Indian savoury snack made from roasted or boiled peanuts dressed with plain yoghurt, finely chopped raw onion, tomato-based sauce, and a homemade chaat masala sauce. The dish belongs to the broad family of Indian chaat preparations — street-food snacks characterised by a balance of tangy, spicy, and creamy flavours. In Singapore, peanut chaat is served at North Indian restaurants such as those in the Boat Quay area and is typically presented as a starter or light snack. Texturally, the dish combines the crunch of roasted peanuts with the creaminess of yoghurt and the sharpness of raw onion. Nutritionally, peanuts are the dominant ingredient and contribute substantial protein (~14 g per 100 g of dish), healthy unsaturated fats, dietary fibre, niacin, folate, vitamin E, phosphorus, and magnesium. The yoghurt component adds calcium and a small amount of vitamin B12. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index (~23) due to the high fat and protein content of peanuts, making it a relatively blood-glucose-friendly snack compared to other chaat varieties. Sodium content is moderate and comes primarily from the sauces and masala seasoning. Peanut chaat is vegetarian and typically halal, but is not suitable for individuals with peanut or dairy allergies.
common_user_queries: Is peanut chaat good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for peanut chaat as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 roasted groundnut data and USDA FoodData Central entries for yoghurt and onion, weighted by approximate ingredient proportions. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database peanut entry using a weighted-ingredient method. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and grounded in approved sources, but actual restaurant preparation proportions and sauce recipes will introduce variability.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 (roasted groundnuts) and USDA FoodData Central (yoghurt FDC #171284, onion FDC #170000) using a weighted-ingredient model (peanuts ~50%, yoghurt ~25%, onion ~10%, sauces ~15% by weight). 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0; this is confirmed zero as peanuts and plain yoghurt contain negligible industrially produced trans fats per USDA FoodData Central data. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in either ICMR-NIN 2017 or USDA FoodData Central for this food combination; peanuts and plain yoghurt are not significant dietary sources of vitamin D and the value is not available from the approved source databases used for this record. 4. GI estimated at 23 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database peanut value and weighted composite method; glycemic load per 200 g serving = 8.4 g. 5. Serving size of 200 g is an estimate based on typical North Indian restaurant chaat portion; actual restaurant portions at field survey-listed venues may vary. 6. The input food_name 'Peanut Chat' is a common anglicised spelling variant; canonical English name corrected to 'Peanut Chaat'. 7. Allergen note: contains peanuts (major allergen) and dairy (yoghurt); not suitable for peanut-allergic or dairy-allergic individuals. 8. Halal status assumed based on typical preparation; individual restaurant certification should be verified.
